item.title: CCP Games Rebrands to Fenris Creations, Focused on 'Long-Term Future of the EVE Universe'
item.content:encoded:
With last week’s announcement that Pearl Abyss had sold CCP Games back to the studio’s leadership team, CCP became independent once more. Today, they announced a rebranding to Fenris Creations, a name tied to the company’s history and values. They’ve also announced a new partnership with Google DeepMind.

The name Fenris Creations is drawn from Norse mythology and something core to the company’s values: 

“When we sought out to make games in 1997 we were grounded by a guiding principle; for a virtual world to have meaning, it must be shaped by risk, loss, renewal, and the people who inhabit it. In short: death is a serious matter.”

With this new era Fenris Creations is “an independent company built to support the long-term future of the EVE universe”, and those big plans continue. This is a transition but not one with layoffs or team changes, the announcement assures us. The EVE Online, EVE Frontier, EVE Vanguard, and EVE Galaxy Conquest teams remain and their studios continue operations. 

The new partnership with Google DeepMind is a research partnership that is “focused on intelligence in complex, dynamic, player-driven systems”, and the draw here was the fact that EVE  already operates like a complex living world. Alexandre Moufarek, Google DeepMind’s director, says “It is a one-of-a-kind simulation for testing general-purpose artificial intelligence in a safe sandbox environment. Their partnership looks to “push the frontier of artificial intelligence and explore new player experiences”.

All of this comes as EVE Online had real momentum heading into 2026, with one of the strongest quarters in the game’s history. With the focus on the long-term, it makes sense coming from this company in particular. Many companies will talk about wanting long-term success without having a 20-year old game successfully running and even breaking records. There is still capacity for surprise and that is also important here.

“New Eden is alive because you keep making it alive”, the announcement says. It will take us all a little time to get used to saying Fenris Creations, but this new chapter is here, to chew over before Fanfest.



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/ccp-games-rebrands-to-fenris-creations-focused-on-long-term-future-of-the-eve-universe-2000137990

item.title: All Of The Best Cosplay From Lvl Up Expo 2026
item.content:encoded:
2026 is truly raising the game for cosplay. After the extraordinary outfits we featured that appeared at WonderCon, we’ve got another extraordinary gallery of incredible costumes from 2026’s Lvl Up Expo, via the photography magic of Mineralblu. The Vegas show, which is all about pop culture and gaming, has cosplay at its core, and people really delivered.

Dispatch was once again featured, alongside some very imaginative takes on the regulars, such as Overwatch, Resident Evil and Minecraft. You’ve never seen an Ender Dragon quite like this one. There are also some exquisite recreations of fits from World of Warcraft and Silent Hill, and the best depiction of a Mii I’ve ever seen.

Oh, and to my absolute delight, the first ever outfit I’ve seen from The Amazing Digital Circus, and incredible YouTube show that my 11-year-old introduced me to. Seriously, it’s incredible stuff, and you’ll also get to judge my parenting for letting him watch it. Cosplayer animasian78 could have chosen far simpler characters to recreate, but instead opted for the malevolent Caine, a set of false teeth with eyeballs inside his mouth. Kudos.

Ah yes, and I’ve completely undersold the appearance of a Expedition 33-cosplaying doggo.

Check out a whole bunch more cosplay in Mineralblu’s video below, and then scroll on down for the best fits.

Right, let’s crack on.

Baguette – Clair Obscur: Expedition 33

Pyramid Head – Silent Hill

U.S.S. Iowa – Kantai Collection

T45 – Fallout

 Emre – Overwatch

Peni Parker – Marvel Rivals

Ender Dragon – Minecraft

Blonde Blazer – Dispatch

Courier 6 – Fallout New Vegas

Husk & the Huskettes – Hazbin Hotel

Grace Ashcroft as Lady Dimitrescu – Resident Evil Requiem

Starlight – The Boys

Iron Man

Rockstar Bonnie – Five Nights At Freddy’s

Caine – The Amazing Digital Circus

Seven – Deadlock

Lady Liadrin – World of Warcraft

Mii – Nintendo



https://kotaku.com/lvl-up-expo-cosplay-clair-obscur-dispatch-marvel-overwatch-fallout-2000693554

item.title: Dying Light Franchise Director Steps Down After 13 Years at Techland
item.content:encoded:
Dying Light’s leadership team has undergone a drastic change today as Tymon Smektala, veteran developer, announces his departure from Techland after thirteen years at the firm. Smektala started with Techland in May 2013, working as a game designer, before moving into a production role on the first Dying Light game.

Smektala departs the company, having most recently served almost four years as the Franchise Director behind the entire Dying Light series, the last deliverable of which role was to push out Dying Light: The Beast.

Tymon Smektala Departs Techland After a Decade

In a post on social media, Tymon Smektala, former Franchise Director of Dying Light at Techland, wrote:

After years with Dying Light, I’m moving into a new chapter. It’s been an incredible journey, and I’m proud of what we’ve built.

Thank you to all the talented people of Techland I’ve had the chance to work with – across both development and publishing – for the passion, energy, and shared ambition to make Dying Light the ultimate zombie experience. We made it happen, together!

And to our amazing community: your love, engagement, and vocal support have always been our North Star. You helped shape what Dying Light became.

The franchise is in great hands, and I’m excited to see it grow even further, and I can’t wait to experience its next chapter as a player. I’ll always root for Dying Light!

Good night, and good luck!

Last year, Techland released Dying Light: The Beast, a whirlwind title that brought Kyle Crane, the game’s first protagonist, back into the limelight. It effectively kickstarted a new chapter of the Dying Light saga, which is what I learned when I sat down with Tymon a year ago in Los Angeles.

That was when he told me that Dying Light: The Beast was being thought of internally as Dying Light 3.

It was also when I learned more about the nugget of a game that was Dying Light: Hellrunners, a title that ultimately never saw the light of day.

Smektala hasn’t indicated what he moves onto, but I’m excited to see where he goes next.

Are you surprised to learn about this big industry move? Let us know your thoughts on the Insider Gaming Discord server.

For more Insider Gaming coverage, check out the news that Build a Rocket Boy has made more layoffs

Senior Editor and Esports Lead

Grant has been gaming for 30+ years and in the industry for 10+. You'll probably find him playing a post-apocalyptic game or an extraction shooter somewhere.

More from Grant Taylor-Hill

MOBILE APP

Level Up Your Gaming News

Real-time news, exclusive podcasts, push alerts, and a better reading experience.

Available on iOS & Android

item.title: EA boasts record year thanks to Battlefield 6 and EA Sports FC
item.content:encoded:
EA has published its preliminary financial results for the fiscal year ended March 31, 2026.

According to a press release, net bookings totalled a record-breaking $8 billion in FY26, up 9 percent year-over-year. Net revenue for FY26 was $7.5 billion, up 1 percent year over year. Battlefield 6's impressive launch and performance were lauded, as were the performances of EA Sports FC 26, FC Online, and FC Mobile. 

Apex Legends earned a mention as well after delivering its "strongest net bookings quarter of the fiscal year in Q4."

"Driven by our talented teams and disciplined execution, we delivered a record FY26, highlighted by the incredibly successful launch of our iconic Battlefield franchise," said EA CEO Andrew Wilson.

"With the recent completion of a debt process that was met with strong investor demand and our ongoing constructive engagement with regulators, we look ahead to closing the transaction and the opportunities it will unlock."

Related:EVE Online studio CCP Games turns independent and rebrands as Fenris Creations

That success comes off the back of job cuts. Earlier this year, EA laid off an undisclosed number of Battlefield developers just months after the record-breaking launch of Battlefield 6.

The company will not be hosting a conference call this quarter due to its pending acquisition by an investor consortium that includes Saudi Arabian sovereign wealth fund, PIF.

Numerous lawmakers and leaders have expressed concern about the take-private deal, including labor union CWA Canada and numerous US congress members.

About the Author

Editor-in-Chief, GameDeveloper.com

Danielle is the editor-in-chief of Game Developer, with previous editorial posts at Fanbyte, VICE, and Polygon. She’s also a lecturer in game design at the Berklee College of Music, and a hobbyist game developer in her spare time.



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/ea-boasts-record-year-thanks-to-battlefield-6-and-ea-sports-fc

item.title: GTA 6's development costs estimated to be over $1bn, as Take-Two head admits its an "expensive" endeavour
item.content:encoded:
GTA 6 parent company Take-Two is estimated to have spent between $1bn and $1.5bn on Rockstar's upcoming game.

That's according to Business Insider (via IGN), which based these staggering figures on estimations made by industry analysts, as part of an interview with Take-Two's head honcho, Strauss Zelnick.

To compare other big 'triple-A' video game releases from recent years, in 2023 it was revealed that Horizon Forbidden West cost $212m (£168m) to develop over five years between 2017 and 2022, with 300 employees attached to the project. At this same time, fellow PlayStation exclusive The Last Of Us Part 2 was revealed to have cost $220m (£174m) to make, with an employee count of roughly 200. More recently, Bungie's Marathon reboot reportedly had a budget of $200m.

So, yes, big games have been costly to develop for quite some time, but it seems that GTA 6 is costly with extra emphasis on each and every letter in the word.

Zelnick himself has stated GTA 6 has been an "expensive" endeavour, though he refrained from putting an actual figure on how much GTA 6 is costing its parent company. Given analysts are talking about a figure over one billion dollars, though, this feels like quite an understatement from the Take-Two head.

But, it is not just GTA 6's development cost that has caused discussion in the industry. Just how much Rockstar and Take-Two will charge for its series' next car boosting romp has also stirred up some discourse, and early last year, a report suggested that GTA 6 could retail for $100.

In April, Zelnick discussed GTA 6's price, and while he didn't give us that all important figure, the Take-Two CEO stressed that the company's "job is to charge way, way, way less of the value delivery". At this time, Zelnick called "making the most spectacular piece of entertainment on Earth, in history" a "pretty daunting challenge".

GTA 6 is currently scheduled to release on 19th November, across PS5 and Xbox Series X/S. For more, here is everything we know about Grand Theft Auto 6 so far.

Mentioned in this article



https://www.eurogamer.net/grand-theft-auto-6-development-cost-billions

item.title: WOLFHOUND Preview - Wolfenvania
item.content:encoded:
Sci-fi Metroidvanias are a dime a dozen, but I can't think of any with a World War 2 setting. That is, until WOLFHOUND. I'm grateful for that, because the genre feels a bit saturated at the moment. Something fresh is needed, and WOLFHOUND might just be the answer.

In WOLFHOUND, you infiltrate a mysterious island in the Bermuda Triangle. The Nazis are embedded in the island, with a large fortress towering over a lush jungle. Seems like a job for Captain Chuck Rossetti, an all-American badass. Turns out, Nazis are just the tip of the iceberg. This island is full of much more than Allied intelligence would suggest.

Captain Rossetti, AKA WOLFHOUND

WOLFHOUND is as authentic a Metroidvania as it gets. It's very old-school, from the 8-bit, retro art style down to the gameplay. Because of this, it's instantly familiar and easy to jump into without learning a bunch of convoluted mechanics; still, despite its simplicity, it's no less fun than that of its counterparts. 

This demo build I previewed comprises two major environments and a few boss battles. Along the way, I collected various upgrades to help me traverse new obstacles and access areas I couldn't reach earlier -- pretty standard fare. What I like is how grounded WOLFHOUND is so far. 

Despite the giant bugs, zombies, and Nazis our character encounters, all of the upgrades thus fit the World War 2 theme. Tactical pads -- you know, knee pads -- are used to roll through smaller gaps. Gotta protect those knees, after all.

Likewise, the climbing gloves allow the Captain to grasp onto ledges and cracks in the wall. Now you're able to scale cliffs and sort of wall jump, given you have the proper handholds. You still feel like a normal dude, not some super soldier in a special suit like Samus Aran or the like. 

And WOLFHOUND promises to provide a number of different WW2-era weapons, as well. You start out with a basic pistol, which has unlimited ammo. Think of this as Samus' blaster, except it requires reloading. Then there's the bolt-action rifle, which has a longer range single projectile with more power to it. I later found a shotgun, but due to its closer range, I didn't find it quite as useful.

Either way, whatever weapon you choose, these can be enhanced further with weapon kits. You can upgrade a weapon's attributes, such as damage, which is great to see -- it means that even the earlier weapons don't fall off later on, so you're able to keep using your preferred guns that cater to your play style. 

The caveat is that you can only hold your sidearm and one larger gun from the start. There's also a second slot for weapons, so presumably, you'll be able to utilize your pistol, bolt-action rifle, and shotgun (or whatever other weapons you find) and switch between the three of them. As I understand it, we'll also gain access to a flamethrower, Thompson, and even a railgun later on. 

A Mysterious Island

Map design was about as well-made as one would come to expect for a Metroidvania. It strikes a nice balance between platforming and enemy placement, keeping me on my toes constantly. Mines are scattered all around the island, and it's easy to miss them when you're focused on that giant bug or Nazi ahead of you.

In one early instance, there's a long, linear path of graves that constantly spawned zombies. At first, this proved troublesome, with the zombies leaping forward and surrounding me quickly. Luring them into the mines was a nice way to avoid some danger, since you'll have to otherwise mag dump on these undead foes. 

All too often, at least for modern Metroidvanias, I find that maps are just one long, linear pathway. Sure, there's some verticality to these maps and hidden areas, but nothing too crazy. WOLFHOUND's developer, Bit Kid, did its homework. This feels like a legit, authentic map that goes in all directions, which is great for those who love exploring and not simply beelining to the next area.

Indeed, I came across a Nazi castle after riding a lift high up cliff, which felt like the natural continuation of the first jungle area. It was a dead end, with a gate barring the way. To access this area, you'll actually have to go below, into these crystal mines full of weird, mutated miners and drill machines.

By navigating this tricky crystal mine -- which, as it turns out, is inside the cliff and reaches far above the jungle but also below it as well -- you're able to get into the underbelly of this castle. Alas, that's where my demo ended, but it was at that point that WOLFHOUND really hooked me. 

WOLFHOUND Preview | Final Thoughts

I'm impressed with WOLFHOUND. From my perception, it doesn't aim to reinvent the wheel. Rather, WOLFHOUND aims to be a competent, well-made Metroidvania that provides a great, nostalgic experience full of challenge and exploration. And so far? It's done pretty well. I'm very excited to see what else it has to offer. 

WOLFHOUND was previewed on PC with a code provided by the publisher over 2 hours of gameplay - all images were taken during the preview process.

Austin cut his teeth writing various  fan-fiction stories on the RuneScape forums when he was in elementary school. Later on, he developed a deep love… More about Austin



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/wolfhound-preview-wolfenvania

item.title: Sam Reich Explains Game Changer: Home Edition's Modular Approach, How To Play, and Why Their Kickstarter Is Also a Game
item.content:encoded:
Yesterday Dropout.tv launched a Kickstarter for Game Changer: Home Edition, a board game version of their popular game show where the gimmick is that the game the contestants are playing changes every episode. With the Game Changer: Home Edition, fans will be able to play Bingo, Name A Number, and Sam Says from the comfort of their own homes.

We got to sit down and talk with Sam Reich, CEO of Dropout and host of Game Changer, just hours after the launch of the Kickstarter to learn more about each of the modular games, how the approach to prompts was different when creating a board game, and how they came up with the community challenges that replace the traditional stretch-goal content unlock format. 

To top off the interview, I had to congratulate Reich on the Kickstarter, which had an initial goal of $40,000, for reaching $1 million in funding in about exactly two hours after launch.

After a long pause of bewilderment, Reich broke the silence, "Thanks, Andy, what else are you supposed to say? It's sincerely hard to comprehend that amount of money, let alone 7,500 people." Reich paused to recheck the Kickstarter page. "Excuse me, 7,745."

Asking how closely he was watching the numbers rise, he simply said: "How could you not take advantage of the dopamine?"

To start at the top of the idea itself, I asked Reich how the Home Edition came to be and how long this had been in the works. "You know, we really aren't die-hard strategists. The reason why now is because we opportunized on figuring this out finally."

Talking on the history of converting Game Changer to a product fans could play at home, Reich said, "For years, we've obviously had the opportunity to do this. The Game Changer fandom has been building a lot over the last few years, but it's a complicated creative challenge to try to turn Game Changer into a home game or party game.

"We had been taking pitches off and on for a while, and I didn't feel like anyone had really nailed it, and then Joshua Balvin comes along and pitches us a version of the game that's very in sync with my ambitions for the game as a business, but also incredibly creatively cohesive. We played through his mock of it as a writers' room and were like, 'Oh my god, there's a real game here.' And then we all started iterating together on prompts, and it was ready now."

Reich chuckled, almost in disbelief. "I mean, that is to say, we really did want the game to be fully realized by the time we launched it on Kickstarter. We didn't want to launch with something half-baked and then figure it out, particularly because we thought, 'If the fans all rush to support this thing, we want to make sure we're going to be able to deliver on it.' Right here, now staring at this $1.2 million number, I'm so happy we did that."

"Knowing that I have something I'm really proud of to be able to share with all these people is super exciting." - Sam Reich

Game Changer: Home Edition Is a Modular Game With Room to Grow

Diving into the game itself, I was interested to learn how each of the modules worked. "It's a modular game, which I was really excited to build because I want this to be something we can continue to invest in.

"This is NOT a one-and-done kinda thing," Reich warned. "In my ideal world, this is a modular game where we can eventually come out with future modules, and folks can basically swap out modules to create their own changing Game Changer experience."

Talking about playing the current version of Game Changer: Home Edition, Reich explained, "A full game takes place over three rounds, with a game per round. This initial launch packet consists of three games, the first of which is Bingo, the second is Name A Number, and the third is Sam Says. All three of those have been reimagined from the show into games that will make sense in party-game atmosphere.

"Bingo is a guess-the-word game where you are trying to get your team to guess the word on your card without saying any of the words that the opposing team has written down that you might also say." Comparing it to a game that a fan of party games might have familiarity with, Reich elaborated, "Imagine you playing a version of Celebrity where you could set off a landmine."

Deeper gameplay strategy immediately comes into play when you're not just playing the game, but the person across from you. "If the opposing team knows you very well, it's all the more likely I'm going to set off those landmines."

"Name A Number is basically a stunt game where you are signing up to do something a certain number of times without knowing what that something is. Once you look at it you can pass it to the opposing team, but once you do, they're going to be able to do it for less or more times depending on what's more challenging."

Some examples of challenges that can be seen on Kickstarter are to "Flip the sand timer. You must flip a coin and get # heads before time runs out" or "Get outside and back inside within # seconds" where # is the number that you or another player proposes.

"Sam Says is a very difficult follow-the-rules type game. With each one of these games, my writers' room has been actively involved in writing prompts, which has been really, really fun to do."

Prompts Crafted to Support Humor

With the prompts being created in his writers' room, I asked, when compared to the standard prompts that are written for Game Changer or similar improv games on Dropout like Make Some Noise, which are crafted for professional improv comedians, how were the prompts for the Home Edition approached?

"It is different," Reich began, "I think the Dropout/Game Changer fandom is funny, but we also want for this game to reach people who don't consider themselves all that funny."

"The burden of comedy has to be more so on us than on the player."

"We also want the players to be able to show up with their creativity, so that's a tough balance. In Game Changer, we primarily are designing games that are fun to watch; it actually doesn't matter if they're that fun to play. They might even be torturous to play… here they have to be primarily fun to play," Reich said.

"There's also the challenge that we did not want to come up with a 'hosted' game. We wanted to come up with a game that everyone could play together. With the host role taken out, that requires some tweaking too. It is in that sense a bit of a translation that takes place into the home game but I really think that we've nailed it. I think it will be evident in the Parlor Room and Smosh Games episodes that are coming out in the next few weeks. It's really fun."

Building to a Big Finale

Knowing that one of my favorite aspects of Game Changer is in the escalation, starting with a bit and making it bigger or even inverting it, I asking if the prompts were set up at all in a way that would cater to that.

With a big smile across his face, Reich explained: "The answer to your question is just 'no.'

"There are more and less challenging prompts for sure. You know, part of the challenge in designing the home game is that you do know the rules when you get started. How we're doing that in a way that remains fun and surprising for the audience, and the answer is that you may know what the rules are, but you don't know what the prompts are that we're asking you to do."

"Particularly in [Sam Says and Name A Number], the prompts are going to ask you to do some pretty outrageous things. Now, if you play Bingo, followed by Name A Number, followed by Sam Says, you are playing in an escalating order of zaniness. So that's the order that we suggest."

"Bingo is the most straightforward, Name A Number is medium to high zany, and then Sam Says is like Name A Number on steroids."

Community Challenges to Unlock New Content

It wouldn't be a Game Changer Kickstarter if the Kickstarter itself weren't a game. Instead of traditional funding goals to unlock additional content, there are 60+ challenges that will be put to the community. Some that we've already seen are for 250 people to comment with a haiku, posting 200+ photos posing as Y, M, C, or A, and recreating the Game Changer logo with edible materials.

"We're working with some talented folks to be able to pull this Game Changer campaign off. Our feeling was that we couldn't in good conscience launch our Game Changer campaign without the campaign itself feeling Game Changer-y in nature, which is ambitious because, on the one hand, we're already launching a game that we had to come up with, and now we're launching challenges related merely to the campaign.

"It's a lot of work. Listen Andy, we just did the work, spoiler alert, there's over 60 of these Kickstarter challenges, which like, if day one is any indication, we're probably going to get through them."

Reich teased that "some of them are meaningfully more challenging," letting the question slip, "Are we going to get a backer in Antarctica? I don't know… but it was really fun coming up with those."

Talking a bit more seriously, Reich said that "fundraising is a little bit of a capitalist exercise, and this helps take some of the harder edges off of that. Where it's like 'Our Stretch Goals are fun,' it's not merely to do with fundraising."

I could definitely understand the want to not just have the campaign be about a big number getting even bigger, and I am sure there are so many Game Changer fans ravenous to take part in a game that Reich is hosting.

Speaking of the secondary benefit of the Community Challenges, Reich also pointed out, "the fact that these will happen will help amplify the campaign. But some of them are like so silly," Reich broke out into laughter.

"We're working with a team called Popular Demand on fulfilling the Kickstarter campaign portion itself and the fine folks at Popular Demand are helping us. They both championed this and also are helping us to actually make sure we run it fairly, as difficult as that will be."

I did warn Reich that he'll have to start preparing for interest in flights to Antarctica to rise as that Community Goal is announced…

Another Community Goal that Reich revealed was "get my dad to donate to the campaign."

"I'm looking forward to the day when tens of thousands of Dropout fans are sicced on him," Reich said.

Sam Reich's father, Robert Reich, who has appeared on Dropout/Game Changer a few times, is a professor, author, lawyer, and political commentator who worked in the administrations of Presidents Gerald Ford and Jimmy Carter, was the Secretary of Labor for President Bill Clinton, and was also a member of the economic transition advisory board for President Barack Obama.

As Reich pulled a glass up to his face to take a sip of water, he added: "I didn't ask for his approval or anything."

As a final nugget of information, as I was thanking Reich for his time and expressing my anticipation to get my hands on the game, Reich told me, "I hope you like it. I'm a big fan of it, and believe it or not… we're already planning Box Two."



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/features/sam-reich-explains-game-changer-home-editions-modular-approach-how-to-play-and
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Eve Online Developer CCP Games Rebrands to Fenris Creations
item.content:encoded:
Eve Online developer CCP Games has rebranded itself to Fenris Creations, and the company has also announced that it's partnering up with Google's DeepMind AI division to "explore new gameplay experiences" powered by the division's technologies.

Per a press release, Fenris Creations, which recently split from former parent company Pearl Abyss after a management buyout, will return to "a model similar to how the company operated before 2018", namely one "designed to support strategic decision-making for persistent live games and long-running virtual worlds".

If you're worried about this change affecting the day-to-day operations of Eve Online, you needn't be, it seems; Fenris says its "leadership team, studios, products, and ongoing development plans" are unchanged, and the same people who have shepherded Eve for many years are still at the helm.

As part of the rebrand, Fenris also shows off some of the projects on which it's currently working, including Eve Online (naturally), as well as space survival sim Eve Frontier and the studio's proprietary Carbon engine.

Another project shown on the banner is Eve Vanguard, described by Fenris as "an extraction-adventure FPS" in which you'll do the usual extraction shooter things, namely exploring, looting, and getting the heck out of Dodge.

Conspicuous by its absence, however, is the shooter announced in conjunction with developer Sperasoft back in 2020. It seems like that project is still missing in action, although it is, of course, possible that we'll hear about it in the coming months.

Alongside its upcoming project slate, CCP-slash-Fenris also revealed a partnership with Google's DeepMind AI division, which the company says "will explore areas including long-horizon planning, memory, and continual learning, using Eve Online as a uniquely rich environment for study".

Google has also acquired a minority stake in Fenris as part of this team-up, although it doesn't seem like that stake will affect the company's creative decisions (not yet, at least).

For now, it seems as though Eve Online will continue to operate as it always has, so this change shouldn't impact you too much if you're a longtime fan. Stay tuned for more.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/eve-online-ccp-games-fenris-creations

item.title: Clair Obscur’s Publisher Explains Why It’s Launching A Physical Gaming Magazine In The Year 2026
item.content:encoded:
Publishers used to be the way that developers got their games manufactured and stocked on store shelves for people to buy. As games have gone digital, the purpose of publishers has expanded and evolved. They can provide financial backing, marketing power, and the resources for QA testing and localization. Publishers can also be markers of taste, signaling which games people should be paying attention to. And few publishers have aimed to cultivate a reputation around hyper-selectivity and high-quality design more than Clair Obscur publisher Kepler Interactive. A new physical magazine called Reset is the label’s latest way of expanding that footprint and elevating the cultural conversation around games and the creativity that goes into making them.

“We always value the analogue and the tactile,” Alexis Garavaryan, CEO of Kepler Interactive, said in a press release. “As the world moves increasingly towards the ephemeral, we wanted to celebrate permanence and legacy. Reset is the embodiment of that, and it was our goal to create something beautiful. An object that can be held, experienced and kept as a lasting point of reference.”

The first issue of Reset will be sold directly by Kepler Interactive through its new online store, with plans to eventually try to get it distributed on physical store shelves as well. It features cover stories on Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 creative director Guillaume Broche, fashion designer Yaku, and artist Mélanie Courtinat, as well as interviews with Yoto Taro (Nier: Automata), Ville Kallio (Cruelty Squad), and others. The essays, conversations, and extended visual treatments position game design alongside more established cultural disciplines and explore the ways in which creators take ideas and inspiration from all walks of life.

“Why would that be not obvious to everyone that video game designers and developers are the same as architects or fashion designers or musicians, and that they all take inspiration from each other and loop in and around?” creative director Simon Sweeney, who was brought on at Kepler three years ago specifically to help launch the magazine, told Kotaku. “Kepler is a space that wants to kind of celebrate that and highlight that…this idea that games could be seen as an art form in the same way as design or architecture or music, and that we were going to champion that primarily, rather than being the insular kind of traditional video game publisher, felt like a no brainer to me.”

reset.

a new kind of video game magazine. pic.twitter.com/aib4ogy9Jj

— Kepler Interactive (@Kepler_Interact) May 6, 2026

Kepler was formed by seven indie studios from all over the world in 2021 and quickly established itself by signing a lineup of eye-catching games like Sifu and Scorn. Garavaryan previously bragged that the publisher evaluates over 1,000 games every year while only picking two or three to work with. The key ingredients for getting Kepler’s attention are innovative game mechanics and a distinct aesthetic. This has encompassed everything from Pacific Drive, a sci-fi survival crafting sim about driving a station wagon through a dangerous Pacific Northwest, to Rematch, a flashy but intuitive third-person soccer game.

Helped by a $120 million investment round from Chinese gaming giant NetEase, the London-based indie label funded the development of last year’s mega hit Clair Obscur, which has since gone on to win almost every gaming award imaginable. This year, Kepler is launching the psychological thriller Ontos, a spiritual successor to the 2015 cult-horror hit Soma, as well as Orbitals, a sci-fi co-op adventure that looks like if Deep Rock Galactic was turned into an anime by Studio Ghibli.

“We’ve reached almost, not a tipping point, but a gathering point of awareness of what games are in the kind of general cultural zeitgeist,” Sweeney said. “It’s not a small industry anymore. The ways in which games are breaking through all over the place shows that quite clearly in terms of film adaptations and things like that.” Now that a generation of people have grown up with gaming as a natural part of the fabric of pop culture, he hopes their “reference points will be a little bit more loose and a little bit more open” when it comes to how different artistic traditions and mediums influence one another.

The electronic music composer Soichi Terada is a perfect example of an artist bridging those divides. He was DJing in the late 80s, went onto create music for games including Ape Escape in the late 90s, and continues to produce albums that mix house and hip-hop influences with retro game sounds. Kepler approached him for Reset but the composer didn’t bite. “I’ve seen him play live a couple of times here in London and in Amsterdam as well,” Sweeney said. “He’s an amazing musician and yeah, he just didn’t understand it I don’t think. I think he was like, ‘it doesn’t seem real.’ So we’ve already got his address and we’re sending him copies showing how real it is.”

Inspiration for the look, feel, and layout of the magazine come from defunct print publications like NGC Magazine but also modern design publications like Kaleidoscope and Capsule. “The dream obviously would be 50 years from now, there’s a bunch of issues of Reset and they tell the story of the things that Kepler was interested in and the spaces that they wanted to kind of explore,” Sweeney said. “We’re interested in the analog and the physical, and that’s where the magazine lies, and we want to make that personal human connection with people.”



https://kotaku.com/clair-obscurs-publisher-explains-why-its-launching-a-physical-gaming-magazine-in-the-year-2026-2000693551

item.title: Does Subnautica 2 Early Access Have Co-op or Multiplayer? Single-Player Status, Explained
item.content:encoded:
Previous Subnautica adventures have been compelling journeys under the sea. One thing that could have made them even more enjoyable (and less terrifying) is getting to explore the water with someone else in co-op multiplayer.

Games have to evolve, right? If you stick to the same time formula for too long without much innovation, then it might have been best not to release something new. Subnautica 2 early access delivers a sprinkling of something new in almost every way, but the addition of any multiplayer would be a game-changer.

Does Subnautica 2 Early Access Have Multiplayer or Co-op?

It’s happening: Subnautica 2 early access features fully-implemented multiplayer AND co-op functionality, allowing up to four people to join you in your travels.

  • More Insider Gaming: Subnautica 2 Early Access Xbox Game Pass Status, Explained

The news many of you have been waiting for in a Subnautica sequel. Here’s the confirmation ripped straight from the Subnautica 2 Discord FAQ:

‘Yes. Subnautica 2 is being designed as a single-player experience that you can optionally play with friends, in up to 4-player co-op.’

You only have to look at the screenshot above to see it in action. Complete four-player online co-op and multiplayer for you to enjoy with your friends. You can add all the new submersibles and fish you want, give the people multiplayer, and they’ll be happy no matter how long Subnautica 2 is in early access.

Can You Play Subnautica 2 Early Access Single-Player?

Subnautica 2 can also be enjoyed as a single-player game, like the previous two titles, if you want to immerse yourself in a solo world.

Just to reiterate, as it’s made clear in the earlier FAQ statement, Subnautica 2 isn’t abandoning its single-player focus. Multiplayer and co-op play are optional, and you’re free to explore the world at your own leisure.

  • More Insider Gaming: Subnautica 2 Early Access Release Date, Times & All Platforms

If you have any doubts about the game’s intent, even the Steam page explains: ‘Subnautica 2 is being crafted as a single-player experience.’

This is the focus, so don’t be worried about multiplayer taking over.

The lawsuit allegations haven’t helped Subnautica 2, but they shouldn’t take away from what is going to be a memorable sequel. Whether you’re excited about the game in general, or specifically the addition of multiplayer, why not talk about Subnautica 2 through our Insider Gaming Discord?

Don’t forget to check out how much Subnautica 2 early access will cost.

Written by

Evergreen Editor

Andy is Insider Gaming's Senior Evergreen Editor and has been in the games journalism sphere in one way or another for over 7 years. His video game taste is as…

More from Andrew Highton

item.title: EVE Online studio CCP Games turns independent and rebrands as Fenris Creations
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

May 6, 2026

3 Min Read

Image via Fenris Creations

EVE Online developer CCP Games is becoming an independent studio and rebranding as Fenris Creations.

The Icelandic company was sold back to its management team by former owner Pearl Abyss in a deal worth $120 million. The South Korean publisher acquired CCP Games in 2018 for around $425 million. 

Now, after regaining its independence, Fenris will operate as a standalone studio responsible for its own strategy, operations, and creative direction. 

"Fenris Creations is now governed by its own Board of Directors, returning to a model similar to how the company operated before 2018, and one designed to support strategic decision-making for persistent live games and long-running virtual worlds," reads an announcement on the Fenris website.

"The ownership group comprises Fenris Creations’ senior management and long-term investors aligned with the company's future as a developer, publisher, and operator of player-driven online experiences."

Related:CWA Canada president says Saudi buyout of EA should trigger 'virtually every alarm bell'

Fenris CEO Hilmar Veigar Pétursson said the studio will not be making layoffs or restructuring as a result of the transition and said EVE Online closed 2025 with some of its best financial results in years after delivering record revenue in November. The company ended the year with over $70 million in reported revenue.

"There are no planned changes to the company’s organizational structure, and its headquarters will remain in Vatnsmýrin, Iceland. Studios in Reykjavík, London, and Shanghai will continue to operate as they do today." the announcement adds. 

Fenris will continue to support the EVE franchise 'with forever in mind'

Notably, Fenris has also entered into a research partnership with Google DeepMind that will see Google acquire a minority stake in the company. The financial terms of the partnership were not disclosed, but Fenris said the duo will focus on "advancing understanding of intelligence in complex, dynamic systems."

"The collaboration will explore areas including long-horizon planning, memory, and continual learning, using EVE Online as a uniquely rich environment for study. Google DeepMind will work with an offline version of EVE Online running on a local server to test and evaluate models in a controlled setting," the announcement continues. 

"Together, the partnership will also explore new gameplay experiences enabled by these technologies."

Deepmind is the artificial intelligence division of Google parent company Alphabet Inc. It has developed a number of generative AI tools such as image generator Nano Banana 2 and world model Genie 3, which Google claims is capable of creating digital worlds that can be explored at will.

Related:Xbox appoints new internal leaders and ditches Copilot AI

 Looking ahead, Pétursson said the EVE franchise is "built to endure" and will continue to be supported over the coming decades. 

"This transition gives us direct ownership, clear accountability, and the independence to invest in worlds that grow over decades,” said Pétursson.

"We're grateful to Pearl Abyss for their partnership and for the consistent support they’ve shown us over the past seven and a half years. EVE Online exists today because of pioneering thinking, patience, and trust between developers and players. Our new structure and partners enable us to carry that legacy forward—continuously evolving a living universe and actively exploring what it can become, with forever in mind."

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/eve-online-studio-ccp-games-turns-independent-and-rebrands-as-fenris-creations

item.title: Halo Infinite gets surprise Gauntlet PvE mode six months after the last planned content update
item.content:encoded:
Halo Infinite's road has been full of ups and downs, but in the end, enough players stuck around to warrant four years of active post-launch development. No more content updates were set to arrive after November 2025, yet the latest multiplayer Halo has just received a new patch which adds a PvE mode.

Firefight: Gauntlet is described as a "deviously difficult PvE mode" designed with the toughest Spartans in mind. Its basic features include "upgradeable attributes, elimination objectives, and auto-scaled difficulty settings to a boss-filled Firefight experience". It's also round-based, and it seems the only way to halt reinforcements is to slay the boss in each arena.

Gauntlet runs are made up of encounters across five different maps "connected by a supply area where you can gather ammo and equipment, roll for power weapons, or level up your personal buffs" before jumping into a new battle. It all sounds slightly inspired by the Call of Duty: Zombies modes if you ask me, and that isn't a bad thing considering the potential player overlap. As a final note, the Halo Waypoint post reveals some of the famous Skull modifiers can be unlocked if the Fireteam is able to "retrieve the Oddball from the Harbinger".

Last November's Operation: Infinite introduced a final operation pass and plenty of cosmetics as Halo Studios' final goodbye to the game. However, it clearly appears some sort of skeleton crew is still tinkering with the Forge tools and coming up with smaller bits of content which are worth adding to the official playlists.

At the time of writing, we know Halo: Campaign Evolved won't feature competitive multiplayer and will instead focus on solo and co-op play, so Infinite might be the shiniest Halo PvP option for a good while still.

Mentioned in this article



https://www.eurogamer.net/halo-infinite-fireteam-gauntlet-pve-mode-update

item.title: Valve release the Steam Controller's CAD files, so you can channel your inner Archie from Balamory and design mods or accessories for it
item.content:encoded:
Take a Steam Controller and this thing I've made to stick to it, and hey, it's a Steam Controller with a thing sticking to it

The Steam Controller's now out in the wild, and it's so nice, we've written some impressions of it twice. If just having the thing in your hands (assuming you grabbed one in time) isn't enough for you, you'll probably be glad to know that Valve have shared the CAD files for it, meaning techies who fancy modding or designing accessories for it can get cracking.

"Today we're making the CAD files for the external shell (surface topology) of Steam Controller and Puck available for download under a Creative Commons license," Valve wrote in a quick announcement post. "This includes an STP model of each, an STL model of each, and an engineering drawing with critical features/keep outs for each."

Dipping into the read me file that comes with the files, which you can find here, Valve added that while "your Steam Controller is yours, and you have the right to do with it what you want", they "highly recommend you leave it to professionals". "Any damage you do will not be covered by your warranty – but more importantly, you might break your Steam Controller, or even get hurt," the Steamyfolks said. "Be careful, and have fun."

So, maybe don't go wild if you're not a seasoned inventor like Archie from classic British kids show Balamory. These files being shared should also result in plenty of unofficial Steam Controller accessories made by other people hitting the shelves at some point soon, and as our very own James has shown in the past, you might not regret buying such things (Disclaimer: You also very much might regret buying such things).

While the Steam Machine's still currently lurking in the priceless ether thanks to RAMnarök, Valve will also be releasing CAD models for that too once it arrives. In that case, custom front panel designs will be the obvious mod of choice out of the gate. After all, who could resisty printing a big picture of Gabe Newell's face, an anime girl, or an anime girl with Gabe Newell's face on the front of their new games box?



https://www.rockpapershotgun.com/valve-release-the-steam-controllers-cad-files-so-you-can-channel-your-inner-archie-from-balamory-and-design-mods-or-accessories-for-it

item.title: Official Assassin’s Creed Account Roasts YouTuber For Posting AI-Altered Leak
item.content:encoded:
Earlier this week, Kotaku reported on the leak from well-known Ubisoft insider j0nathan, who claimed that playtests for a long-rumored PvP-style Assassin’s Creed title, codenamed “Invictus,” had been held in late April. Well, j0nathan is back with a new Assassin’s Creed Invictus leak, but said leak isn’t being as well-received as the last one…mostly because the official Assassin’s Creed X account put him on blast for using AI to “heavily” alter the image featured in the leak.

Last night, j0nathan posted what he claimed was an image from Assassin’s Creed Invictus’ latest playtest on X. Writing in French, j0nathan said, “A big part of the game isn’t textured yet, so don’t be surprised by the big gray blocks—it’s really just to give you a glimpse of what the game looks like right now.” The heavily watermarked accompanying image shows a character from the playtest standing in a coliseum.

Nice try… This might have started as an image from our private test, but it’s been heavily altered (most probably with AI). Not great to spread misinformation. 👎

For those genuinely curious about the project: we'll share more when the time is right!

— Assassin's Creed (@assassinscreed) May 5, 2026

Just under two hours later, j0nathan’s post received a reply from the official Assassin’s Creed X account. “Nice try… This might have started as an image from our private test, but it’s been heavily altered (most probably with AI). Not great to spread misinformation,” reads the reply.

After some grandstanding about how Ubisoft had “totally screwed themselves” in the replies, j0nathan then posted the original, unaltered screenshot from Assassin’s Creed Invictus’ playtest “without AI.” Although he stated that the unsloppified version was “exactly the same crap,” it’s clear that whatever AI tool he used to alter it actually changed quite a lot.

Ok vu que le compte officiel fait le malin, voici le vrai screen sans IA (et malheureusement pour eux, c'est exactement la même merde 😂) https://t.co/9tJkwjQWid pic.twitter.com/V7gMtYuP99

— j0nathan (@xj0nathan) May 5, 2026

For a start, the sky no longer looks like the default Windows XP wallpaper in the original version. The crowd looks less janky, too, and the player character is now standing on a more defined platform. Oh, and yeah, the outfit in the original image is now white, instead of the garish blue and scarlet red fit that the AI slop version is sporting.

While j0nathan seemingly sees this as some kind of victory, as he stated that Ubisoft has “just clearly confirmed” the leak and therefore bolstered his “credibility,” he’s also spent the better half of the past day arguing with people in the replies about getting ratio’d by the Assassin’s Creed X account. At least he can say he’s been embarrassed by the same X account that ratio’d Elon Musk last year. What’s the opposite of being in good company?



https://kotaku.com/assassins-creed-invictus-leak-ubisoft-twitter-j0nathan-playtest-ai-2000693557

item.title: 007 First Light Nintendo Switch 2 Release Could be Delayed Again
item.content:encoded:
The IO Interactive CEO has revealed that the 007 First Light Switch 2 version’s release could be delayed further, but it is coming for sure.

007 First Light’s Switch 2 port was first delayed indefinitely, and then IO Interactive revealed that it would arrive later this summer. However, it now seems the game will be delayed further so the developers can provide the best possible experience for players on Nintendo’s platform.

007 First Light Is Already Running on the Switch 2

While talking to The Game Business, IO Interactive’s CEO has addressed a possible second delay for the 007 First Light Switch 2 version. When asked about it’s status, Hakan Abrak, stated that the game is already running on the Switch 2, however, they don’t plan to release it until they “make it as good as it can be”. The CEO is not willing to hear criticisms of this variant, such as players stating that “it wasn’t a good version.”

Abrak added, that they “need a bit more time to get it where we want it to be.” The developers had first stated Summer 2026, but it seems that it will be pushed to late summer. However, the CEO is confident that the Switch 2 version will come. Abrak concluded, Bond has a special place on Nintendo. And I promise I will do everything I can, and I can do quite a lot being the CEO, to get that in a great shape.”

In other news, the CEO has also stated that a sequel for the 007 First Light franchise depends on public reception. Additionally, IO Interactive’s CEO has confirmed that their new online fantasy RPG IP, Project Fantasy, is in a safe place. What are your thoughts on 007 First Light’s Switch 2 release being delayed further? Leave your thoughts down in the comments, and join the official Insider Gaming Discord server.

For more information from Insider Gaming, read about MindsEye developers laying off more staff post DLC launch. Don’t forget to sign up for our weekly newsletter.

item.title: WheelMates demo now available for PC
item.content:encoded:
• PC

Guide tiny RC cars through a secret-riddled house.

A demo for cooperative RC adventure game WheelMates is now available for PC via Steam, developer Firevolt announced.

Here is an overview of the demo, via Firevolt:

Following the first playtest earlier this year, the doors to the mysterious scientist’s house open up for a public demo, accessible to everyone without waiting. After learning the controls and using the first power-ups in the Kitchen, explore the Garage. This big, open space full of hidden paths offers new tools to use, and more secrets to discover. Unleash gear like magnet grab and rope swing to solve puzzles together, find secret areas, and discover the whereabouts of the professor.

Explore these areas together with a friend, now with split-screen local cooperative play in addition to online play. Drift around corners, performing stunts in the air, and test reworked physics making driving more intuitive and fun. Additionally, the demo adds improved camera control, and new localization for 14 languages in total.

With no timers and no pressure to move fast, WheelMates emphasizes shared problem solving at the duo’s own preferred pace, enabling discovery of surprises and secrets.

WheelMates is due out for PlayStation 5, Xbox Series, and PC via Steam in 2026.

Watch a new trailer below.

item.title: CCP Games rebrands to Fenris Creations following split from Pearl Abyss
item.content:encoded:
Gogle DeepMind partnership announced.

EVE Online developer CCP Games has rebranded to Fenris Creations following its split from former parent company Pearl Abyss, the studio announced. It has also entered into a research partnership with Google DeepMind, focused on advancing understanding of intelligence in complex, dynamic systems.

“EVE is built to endure—and it only works if you’re willing to keep pushing into the future,” said Fenris Creations CEO Hilmar Veigar in a press release. “This transition gives us direct ownership, clear accountability, and the independence to invest in worlds that grow over decades. We’re grateful to Pearl Abyss for their partnership and for the consistent support they’ve shown us over the past seven and a half years. EVE Online exists today because of pioneering thinking, patience, and trust between developers and players. Our new structure and partners enable us to carry that legacy forward—continuously evolving a living universe and actively exploring what it can become, with forever in mind.”

Google DeepMind co-founder and CEO Demis Hassabis added, “Games have always been a huge part of my life—I’ve been a gamer since I was a kid, and I started my career designing and programming complex AI simulation games like Theme Park. They’ve also been at the heart of many of Google DeepMind’s breakthroughs – like Atari DQN, AlphaGo, AlphaStar and SIMA – because they’re the perfect training ground for developing and testing AI algorithms. I’ve known Hilmar for many years and long admired his work, and I’m thrilled to partner with him and the fantastic team at Fenris Creations to explore new gaming experiences and advance AI research safely inside a player-driven universe as amazingly complex as EVE Online.”

Fenris Creations chairman of the board, and Omega Ventures co-founder and partner Birgir Mar Ragnarsson said, “I’ve followed CCP’s progress closely over the past several years and have great respect for what the company has achieved. As Chairman, I’ve seen firsthand how this company performs when its leadership is set up to focus and execute. We’re entering this next phase from a position of strength, with experienced leadership and strong partners in place. I’m looking forward to playing a part in that journey and supporting Fenris Creations as it continues to make history.”

Get the details below.

Independence and Rebranding

Fenris Creations is now governed by its own Board of Directors, returning to a model similar to how the company operated before 2018, and one designed to support strategic decision-making for persistent live games and long-running virtual worlds. The ownership group comprises Fenris Creations’ senior management and long-term investors aligned with the company’s future as a developer, publisher, and operator of player-driven online experiences. As disclosed in Pearl Abyss’ regulatory filings, the transaction value is $120 million USD, with consideration comprising both cash and non-cash elements.

The change reflects a shift in ownership and governance only. Fenris Creations will continue to operate as a standalone studio, responsible for its strategy, operations, and creative direction. Fenris Creations’ leadership team, studios, products, and ongoing development plans remain unchanged, with the same people who have guided the EVE universe for many years continuing to lead the company.

For employees and the local community in Iceland, Fenris Creations emphasized that this transition does not involve restructuring or layoffs. There are no planned changes to the company’s organizational structure, and its headquarters will remain in Vatnsmýrin, Iceland. Studios in Reykjavík, London, and Shanghai will continue to operate as they do today.

Since 2018, CCP Games and Pearl Abyss have worked closely together while evolving as companies. Following a joint review of long-term strategy, both parties concluded that the company is now best supported through independent ownership as Fenris Creations, while Pearl Abyss continues to focus on the growth of its own core titles and IP portfolio. Broader differences in operating context, current strategic focus, and long-term priorities were among several factors considered in this decision.

Pearl Abyss and Fenris Creations part ways with mutual respect following a partnership that supported the company’s growth during an important period in its history. Both companies now move forward with clear direction on their respective priorities.

Google DeepMind Partnership

Alongside this transition, Fenris Creations is entering into a research partnership with Google DeepMind, focused on advancing understanding of intelligence in complex, dynamic systems. The collaboration will explore areas including long-horizon planning, memory, and continual learning, using EVE Online as a uniquely rich environment for study. Google DeepMind will work with an offline version of EVE Online running on a local server to test and evaluate models in a controlled setting. Together, the partnership will also explore new gameplay experiences enabled by these technologies.

Google has invested in Fenris Creations as part of this transition, taking a minority stake in the company.

The Future of the EVE Universe

Fenris Creations enters this next phase with strong momentum across its sci-fi universe. EVE Online closed 2025 with some of its best results in several years, including a record-breaking revenue month in November and a standout Q4 that became the second-highest revenue quarter in the game’s more than 20-year history. The iconic spaceship MMORPG exceeded internal expectations and demonstrates sustained strength as a live service in its third decade of continuous operation. Adding to this strong position are two upcoming titles in development: EVE Vanguard, an extraction-adventure first-person shooter, and EVE Frontier, an online space survival game. The company remains profitable, with over $70M in reported revenue for 2025 and strong reserves, reflecting sustained player engagement and enabling continued investment in the long-term health of the EVE universe.



https://www.gematsu.com/2026/05/ccp-games-rebrands-to-fenris-creations-following-split-from-pearl-abyss

item.title: Poll: How Do You Pronounce These Pokémon?
item.content:encoded:
After 30 years and well over 1000 Pokémon in the National Dex, coming up with snappy names for Pocket Monsters is tough. Add in all the languages the games are localised into, and potential issues around certain words and puns which don't translate well, and it's no wonder that people have different ways of saying certain 'mon.

Some are easy, but all it takes is a couple of vowels to throw things open to interpretation. Stir in regional pronunciation conventions depending on your mother tongue (and, in the case of English, its hodgepodge of inconsistent pronunciation and spelling) and there are loads of ways to pronounce some of the best-loved, well-known Pokémon.

So today, we're asking how you say the names of 22 Pokémon that are often pronounced differently. If you're after official pronunciation, Serebii has a handy, up-to-date list showing how The Pokémon Company itself says these names, and we've added our interpretation of the official versions below each entry.

So, in alphabetical order, let's see how the majority pronounces the Pocket Monsters below...

Note. It's worth mentioning that we covered a selection of divisive Pokémon names many moons ago. Rather than redirecting you to another article for those, we've incorporated the vintage polls below. If you voted before, thank you! Your votes will still be registered.

Also, using the International Phonetic Alphabet below would be ideal, but not all readers will be familiar with that. Therefore, all the phonetic spellings below are approximate, with the stressed syllable denoted by the capital letter...

Arceus

Kicking things off, we looked at the big A's pronunciation a few years back:

Mythical, but also Normal, Arceus is a spiky, horsey-looking thing which also manages to baffle onlookers with its name.

Official pronunciation: Ark-ee-us

Alomomola

And we covered this palindromic Pokémon in the past, too:

Amola... Alomalo... Alamo... This word has metathesis written all over it.

Official pronunciation: uh-Loh-muh-Moh-luh

Chinchou

A final vintage poll before we get into the new ones (that's the way the cookie crumbled going alphabetically!):

This Water / Electric-type looks simple enough but throws in a curveball with that final 'ou'. Chin-chuu? Chin-chow (rhymes with 'OW')? Chin-cho (rhymes with 'show')?

Official pronunciation: Chin-chow

Hydreigon

We start the new batch with Gen V's Dark/Dragon-type. The 'drei' in the middle comes from the German for 'three' (with its previous two forms containing 'eins' and 'zwei'), but how do you say it in English?

Official pronunciation: hai-Drai-gon

Illumise

Jeez Louise, it's Illumise! Or should that be Jeez Loui-say, it's Illumise! Or perhaps...

Official pronunciation: ee-loo-Mee-zay

Linoone

This one presents questions due to that double-O and no-one (or, perhaps, noone) can agree on if it's split or not.

Official pronunciation: lai-Noon

Kyogre

Something about this Gen III Legendary makes us want to swap the 'o' and 'g' around, for some reason.

Official pronunciation: kai-Oh-guh

Kyurem

Sticking with the 'k's and the Legendaries, we're not sure if this one's a key or a cure.

Official pronunciation: Kyoor-rem

Mienshao

A Gen V fighter, this is obviously mee-Enn-shao... or that's what we thought until we heard other suggestions...

Official pronunciation: Meen-shau

Nihilego

Well, the last bit's got to be 'lego', right? Or is that just our bias for Danish plastic coming through?

Official pronunciation: nai-uh-Lee-go

Quaquaval

The watery Gen IX starter evo is appropriately slippery when it comes to its name.

Official pronunciation: Kwak-wuh-vul

Rattata

What's this? A Gen I mainstay!? Surely everyone says this the same! It's like frittata, no?...

Official pronunciation: Ra-tat-ta

Rayquaza

Another Legendary, we always liked to imagine him as Ray Quasar, otherworldly '70s lounge singer.

Official pronunciation: ray-Kway-zuh

Regice

This one's made of ice (ice, baby), so the correct pronunciation would seem clear. Reg-Ice, like 'choc-ice', no? Would that it were so simple...

Official pronunciation: Redge-ice

Reuniclus

Oof, buckle up for options with this Gen V Psychic-type

Official pronunciation: ree-yoo-Nee-klus

Shiinotic

We can only think of two real variants for this Gen VII mushroom.

Official pronunciation: shee-Nah-tik

Silvally

This is sil-Valley. Definitely. 100%... Er, 90% 80%...?

Official pronunciation: sil-Val-ai

Sudowoodo

This Rock-type mixes 'u', 'oo', and 'o' in the same word, highlighting just what an illogical nightmare mess pronunciation can be. Unless you're mixing 'doe' (as in deer, a female deer) and 'do' (as in 'What did you do?') in the same word, there's really only two ways Sudowoodo can go.

Official pronunciation: Soo-doe-Woo-doe

Suicune

[Here] we've got Suicune, the Legendary Water-type. The troublesome 'C' and 'E' complicate matters with this one depending on your mother tongue.

Official pronunciation: Swee-koon

Xatu

Going back to Gen II, we've got a case of the Professor Xaviers...

Official pronunciation: Zah-too

Yveltal

A Dark / Flying beastie, it likes to throw opponents off their guard as they try to pronounce its name. Evil-tell? Yevel-tel? Yu-vetal? Novotel? Oh, it just incinerated us with a Hyper Beam.

Official pronunciation: ee-Vel-tal

Zoroark

And having gone from A to Z (hmm, should have gone from Z to A...), we finish up with another Gen V tongue-twister. How many syllables are you going with?

Official pronunciation: Zor-oh-ark

And there we are. Let us know in the comments if there are any other 'mons you find to be particularly divisive when it comes to PokéPron.

And as for the word 'Pokémon' itself?! Some people say that weird, right? Well, we asked that question long, long ago, too, but let's embed the poll again here for posterity:

How do you say 'Pokémon'? (1,367 votes)

  1. "Poker-mon"13%
  2. "Poke-y-mon"34%
  3. "Pockee-mon"13%
  4. "po-Kay-mon"33%
  5. Other7%

Please login to vote in this poll.



https://www.nintendolife.com/features/poll-how-do-you-pronounce-these-pokemon

item.title: The Secret of Weepstone Reimagines Classic D&D In This Black And White Dungeon Crawling Adventure - Interview
item.content:encoded:
Dungeons & Dragons-inspired video games have been all the rage as of late, from sweeping epics like Baldur's Gate 3 to the new worlds of Solasta. Having played the demo of The Secret of Weepstone, a new indie D&D-inspired game by Talesworth Game Studio, I was excited to sit down with Sean Gailey to discuss the development of The Secret of Weepstone and his inspirations.

To start at the top, I wanted to ask what the game is, but instead got a bit of a cryptic response on that secret being more than just a title. "Well, that's something you'll have to figure out, I guess," Gailey responded with a smirk on his face.

"As the game starts out, the lord [Oswald] of the town of Weepstone sends the party to figure out what's going on and why his brother is being tormented. They grew up nearby in Weepstone Keep, and they believe that's the source of the problem. They long abandoned the keep when they were kids for reasons you'll learn as the story goes on." 

Oswald asks for six volunteers, "Please go out and figure out what's going on, help my brother," and that's the secret that you have to figure out.

Joking about the many secrets of Weepstone that the players will be able to uncover, I shared my love of games that force me to bring out a corkboard, pad, and pen like Tunic or Animal Well. Gailey had me excited, wishing for me to have "lots of strings and tacks on that corkboard when you're done playing this game."

The Origins Of Weepstone

The Secret of Weepstone is self-professed to be inspired heavily by classic D&D. I asked Gailey about his experience with D&D and what about it drew him to crafting this game.

"I grew up playing D&D in the late '70s/early '80s. Basic/Expert was my first D&D, and what really drew me to that was the artwork. The black-and-white art style of the early books really painted what D&D and fantasy looked like to me. The art of Trampier with his Goblin in the Monster Manual, that is what a Goblin looks like for the rest of my life."

"I always dreamed about making a game that would be like stepping into one of those books. One of the things when you're the Dungeon Master, which I typically was growing up, is that there's so much art in the modules that the player never gets to see. When I was playing, I would be trying to cover things up and show the players the artwork. There were some modules like Tomb of Horrors or the Shrine of Tamoachan where you would get these artbooks, and you could show the players art."

Gailey lamented that these art books weren't more popular, musing that it probably wasn't possible for budget reasons. "That was one of the best things about it, the early, early artists, a lot of them were still new and cutting their teeth. The art isn't perfect, it's not great, they couldn't spend forever on it, so it had to be done quicker and on a budget, and that produced something that felt a little bit like art that you would doodle in your notebook. I loved the imperfections.

"The art also brought challenges to these artists; the art had to be read in small format, they could only be black and white, and that poses a lot of challenges to the artists when they were tasked with creating something and painting a scene that evoked emotion. We have the same challenges when it comes to developing Weepstone. The game is just about all black and white, and that creates a lot of challenges from a game design perspective, from a UI perspective.

"Rather than taking the easy way out when we encounter those challenges, we sit with them until we find something creative. It took a long time to even get it to the prototype stage, but I finally feel like the look will not be changing from now until we launch. A lot of the game systems are still being refined."

Weepstone Isn't a Relationship Simulator, It's a Dungeon Crawl

Having played the demo of Weepstone through a few times, I was really liking how it focused on the dungeon crawl of D&D instead of the grander adventures that some other CRPGs with far larger budgets had recently done.

"D&D now, CRPGs now, they're not better or worse… they're different. I think back to a lot of the great concepts of gaming that went away and came back. I remember when permadeath and hardcore mode re-entered the gaming market. Games were getting easier, easier, easier, and then I don't remember what game it was, but this concept of having a hardcore mode is in every game, but for a while we didn't have that.

"One of the goals for Weepstone was that D&D played a lot differently in the early days, and you'd lose characters, and that was part of the fun. You couldn't reload in tabletop, but the concept of character death is a fun part of role-playing games. It's present for our current tabletop games, but the current iteration of D&D, no one ever really dies, and if you do, you can come back easily."

Talking specifically to the type of CRPG/D&D experience that he's wanting to portray with Weepstone, Gailey explained, "I want to bring this old dungeon crawly, the story is pulpy, there's not a ton of dialogue. It's just a different way to play, and that's the experience I'm hoping to bring."

Death can really be sudden in Weepstone, too. In my first playthrough, I immediately killed one of my party members by having them jump into a dark latrine, which resulted in them falling 70 feet to their death.

What this did reveal to me was the Mortal Favor mechanic. Upon the death of a character, a boon passes on to the rest of the party.

"Whenever anyone dies in Weepstone, and no one really knows why, they convey these boons upon their friends and loved ones and it's different for everybody."

After losing Crig, the homeless man living in a dark corner of the abandoned keep, my party earned the Mastery of None, which gives all characters an additional 3% to succeed on their Ability Tests. Another I got was that for the next three times characters in my party would die, they would instead be left on 1 HP.

Gailey talked about how the Mortal Favors wasn't just a way to help boost the party mechanically, but it's also a way to encourage players to not just think of death as a negative and to be okay losing some party members along the way.

"I know that there are going to be players that do want to reload, I'm always a 'play the way you want,' but if I'm doing my job correctly, then you don't want to reload."

Talking about difficulty and approaches to playing, Gailey did allude to different difficulty modes that will be in the final version of Weepstone, but he did highlight that they didn't want to include modes where you can't ever save or have limited saves. While players might like the challenge, Gailey had the feeling that making saving rarer and reloading more of a chore would impede the overall experience.

"I want it to be fun to lose characters and have the upside be enough that you'll want to keep going."

Losing people will also be balanced with the number of people that you'll be able to add to your party as you continue to explore Weepstone Keep and return to the village. That being said, Gailey also highlighted that the party size can go up to 8, so there's plenty of room to grow and shrink.

Combat in Weepstone Doesn't Need a Text Log

Another aspect of D&D that was streamlined in Weepstone is combat. "I've heard a lot of feedback and comments that they enjoyed having a simplified experience. Everything that you need is shown right there on the screen, and that was a design goal. Let's avoid having a text log and not needing one, keeping it simple."

Gailey also highlighted the difficulty of balancing simple with simplistic. Remarking that, as you get further into the game, they were seriously looking at making sure it didn't get too easy. Looking to add to the original D&D formula, there are also additional items like spell scrolls, which will give players a little bit more magic.

Gailey also wanted to very clearly get across the idea that "Weepstone is not a combat engine. A lot of current CRPGs, the center of the game is combat, and that is not… in fact, that's a bad idea when you're playing. When you have a bunch of low-level nobodies in Basic/Expert, you don't want to be in every combat."

"Combat is straightforward, and a little bit stripped down, but we'll see where it is when it launches."

Knowing how unfavorable the dice can be, I was interested to know what a TPK would look like in Weepstone. Would it eject you from the level and force you to scramble together a new party? Without missing a beat, Gailey joked, "It just uninstalls the game, actually."

Following up with a more serious response, when being faced by a TPK, you'll be forced to restart from a prior save. 

Progression in The Secret of Weepstone

Having picked a party of 'nobodies,' set foot in Weepstone keep, and exploring for an hour or so, I was definitely hooked on the gameplay, but I was interested to hear what the rest of the game has in store for continued exploration as well as character growth.

"Leveling up is in the game, that happens on the character sheet. Following Basic/Expert rules for leveling up, so that is a little bit of a concern for me. In practice, it may feel like not enough is happening at that level up to where it is exciting, that's a bit part of tabletop, the feel of power increases, but it's still in that you'll get more HP, you'll get access to more attacks and spells."

"When you first level up to Level 1, which will probably happen midway through the first level that you play through. Since you start at Level 0, there was a bit of a challenge so I just split up the first level into 1000 exp for each class to get to the first level, and you have latent abilities, latent magic user abilities, and latent Cleric abilities when you're Level 0. When you level up, all of those things activate."

Talking more about time outside the dungeon-crawling gameplay, Gailey told me that there would be plenty of chances to return to the town of Weepstone to rest up, swap out your party, and progress the story. There was emphasis that Weepstone and the surrounding lands weren't massive in scale like Middle-earth, but the Kingdom of Talesworth and some of the lore actually all originates from a book he started to write 20 years ago.

While very different in look, The Secret of Weepstone takes place in the same kingdom as Gailey's previously released Talesworth games: World of Talesworth and Talesworth Adventure: The Lost Artifacts.

"It's a pretty big deal what's going on, but it's not a world thing."

The Secret Of Weepstone Release Window

To wrap up our discussion, I was interested to prod to see if there was a smaller launch window than the 2026 that the Steam page is currently showing. Gailey explained that with the amount of interest and wishlists from Steam, it has really set a fire under those working on the game to make sure it's perfect before sending it out.

"When we were approaching, and then hit 100K wishlists, it's become more and more apparent that this needs to be just done right. We don't want to rush it. You know I'm the sole coder; we do have a very small team. There are lots of great collaborators, great artists, Hunter is writing it, my brother Matt is doing a lot of the level design, and then we have a lot of great artists contributing to it."

"We want to make sure we do it right. Once we get closer, we'll be able to give an official date."

Talking more about the wishlists, Gailey revealed his original hopes for engagement on the Steam platform. "I remember in the beginning, before we even launched the demo or Steam page, my goal was 50k wishlists, so to hit 100k and still have quite a ways until launch, I'm so happy that there is an appetite for a game like this.

"The pressure's on now, I really want to deliver something that is fun for everybody, but I also know when you're playing with nostalgia and memories. The demo is a great example of what this game is. If you like the demo, you're going to like the full game."

The Secret of Weepstone is releasing for PC sometime this year, and there's a demo currently available on Steam that will take you ~45 minutes to get through, where you'll get a bit of the story, a lot of exploration, and some combat. Be sure to go wishlist it too!



https://techraptor.net/gaming/features/secret-of-weepstone-reimagines-classic-dd-in-this-black-and-white-dungeon-crawling
