item.title: MindsEye Developer Build a Rocket Boy Hit by Yet More Layoffs
item.content:encoded:
It looks like MindsEye studio Build a Rocket Boy has laid off yet more of its employees, leaving just a relative handful of staff members left to work on the company's ongoing projects.

According to a Kotaku report, which cites "sources familiar with the [latest] restructuring", 170 Build a Rocket Boy employees have lost their job in this round of redundancies, with just around 80 staff members remaining at the studio.

This is, as Kotaku points out, the third time that Build a Rocket Boy has engaged in layoffs in the last twelve months or so. Following the disastrous launch of MindsEye in June last year, the company laid off more than 100 employees, and another round of staff cuts took place in March.

Studio CEO Mark Gerhard blamed the latest round of cuts on "corporate sabotage", a line he's consistently taken in order to explain MindsEye's poor reception. Gerhard appears to believe that MindsEye's spate of negative reviews was caused by a concerted espionage effort rather than, you know, the game not being very good.

In case you missed all of this, MindsEye was released in June last year, and its launch was messy, to say the least, with influencer partnerships being dropped on day one and irate customers demanding refunds from platforms (which were granted in many cases).

The game's launch was so bad, in fact, that it reportedly prompted publisher IO Interactive to exit the third-party publishing game altogether. Build a Rocket Boy split with IO Interactive earlier this year, choosing instead to self-publish MindsEye going forward.

Build a Rocket Boy's company culture doesn't appear to be particularly savory, either; a group of employees recently filed a lawsuit against the studio, alleging unauthorized surveillance.

If you want to check out MindsEye to see what all the fuss is about, it's available now on PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S, although our very own Sam would caution against doing so for all but the stoutest of heart.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/build-a-rocket-boy-layoffs-again

item.title: IO Interactive’s Project Fantasy Is In a “Very Healthy Space”
item.content:encoded:
IO Interactive’s CEO has informed players that Project Fantasy is still in development and remains financed.

Project Fantasy is a new IP in development at IO Interactive. It is an online fantasy RPG that features a world and a game built from the ground up to entertain players and expand for many years to come. There were rumors that the game might have lost its finances, but that doesn’t seem to be the case.

IO Interactive CEO Didn’t Reveal the Partner for Project Fantasy, But Confirms It’s Safe

In a new interview with The Game Business, IO Interactive CEO Hakan Abrak briefly discussed Project Fantasy’s status. Abrak first stated that he won’t reveal the partner and will not confirm or deny whether Xbox is involved in the project. However, what he could confirm was that “Project Fantasy is in a very healthy place, it’s still financed, and it’s amazing.” So any players who were waiting for this project can rest easy.

For those wondering about the game, we don’t know many details, but the developers have said it feels familiar, yet IOI is also on a journey unlike any we have been on before. The developers have also stated how they ended up exploring this genre:

For many, this journey is also a deeply personal one that began long before we started making games for a living – and it started with the fantasy genre. From the “Fighting Fantasy” books, compelling you to choose your path, alone, against wizards, lizards, and thief kings. To the togetherness, camaraderie, agony, and delight found around the tabletop. For some, it meant taking the role of a game master: Part storyteller, part AI opponent, part guide, part villain. For everyone around that table, it meant creativity, imagination, building a world together, and a shared goal in creating a great game experience.

In other news, IO Interactive and Build a Rocket Boy’s publishing collaboration for MindsEye has concluded. Additionally, IO Interactive CEO Hakan Abrak spoke about the future of the 007 franchise and whether a sequel is on the cards. What are your thoughts on Project Fantasy being in a “very healthy space”? Leave your thoughts down in the comments, and join the official Insider Gaming Discord server.

For more information from Insider Gaming, read about the new Worms game being leaked in a bizarre way. Don’t forget to sign up for our weekly newsletter.

item.title: Renowned comedian Matt Berry helped create "lots of fun Bane bits" in Lego Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight
item.content:encoded:
British comedian Matt Berry is voicing Bane in TT Games' upcoming release, Lego Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight, and it sounds like he will be bringing an especially mischievous twist to the iconic DC villain.

Berry is known to many as the IT Crowd's bombastic and rather inappropriate boss, as well as What We Do In The Shadows' vampire Leslie "Laszlo" Cravensworth. More recently, the actor also starred in Amazon's acclaimed Fallout TV adaptation, where he voiced Mr. Handy/Snip Snip.

Talking to Eurogamer at a recent preview event for Lego Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight, executive producer Matt Ellison spoke about working with Berry, calling the experience "really cool" and the actor himself "excellent".

"It was good fun being in the session with [Berry]," Ellison told me when I said I recognised Bane's in-game voice immediately. "There are lots of fun 'Bane bits' that we have in the game," he continued, mentioning prank calls the villain makes to Batman in the Batcave as a particular highlight.

Berry's voice "works really well as Bane", Ellison continued, confirming that the actor was very skillful when it came to improvising and embracing his character's wicked but still humorous side in the voice over booth. A large proportion of What We Do in the Shadows is improvised, so Berry has had plenty of experience in this art. "It was really good fun to have him," Ellison said in closing.

Lego Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight is the latest and most thorough brickified retelling of Bruce Wayne's story. It is due to release later this month, on 22nd May, across PS5, Xbox Series X/S and PC (Steam, Epic). It will also come to Switch 2 at a later date.

I recently went hands-on with Lego Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight, and thoroughly enjoyed my time wandering between missions and the Batcave. "I would go so far as to affectionately dub it Arkham for kids," I wrote in Eurogamer's Lego Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight preview, praising the game's open world Gotham, its more sophisticated combat mechanics and - of course - its humour.

Mentioned in this article



https://www.eurogamer.net/lego-batman-legacy-dark-knight-matt-berry-bane

item.title: The Shore: Enhanced Edition Review – Cthulhu goes to the beach
item.content:encoded:
Releasing on Steam half a decade ago, the original version of The Shore was a solid and enjoyable walking simulator with a focus on atmosphere and spooky Lovecraftian shenanigans, updated to include more explicitly action-based sequences – a choice that met with mixed reception. Now that it’s finally come to consoles, how does it fare? Is this a glimpse into madness that will leave you screaming for more?

You play as a shipwrecked mariner who has lost his daughter, whether due to absence or death is unclear at first. This parental trauma opens him up to the whims of the cosmos but I generally prefer Mythos stories where curiosity – whether academic or predatory – causes the descent into madness. As you explore the beaches and environment you’ll find mysterious messages in bottles that speak of both the history of the island and your own. If anything, though, this can tend towards too much overt exposition and a purple prose that feels influenced by some of the wordier aspects of Lovecraft’s oeuvre.

To make matters worse, The Shore feels like two very distinctly different games crudely stitched together. All of the good work built up by the dread and atmosphere of the first half is squandered by a series of frustrating chase sequences defined by trial and error and getting stuck on the scenery. In this way it reminded me greatly of Conarium, a title I reviewed here a full seven years ago. I’m not sure why Lovecraftian games seem so drawn to stalker enemies and maze escapes when the true horror should be incalculable and uncaring, but I guess that’s a matter of game design and insecurities around the reception to walking simulators.

Perhaps the most egregious example of this comes after an encounter with a vision of Cthulhu, where you jump into a pit and are immediately attacked by a Shoggoth. You’ll almost certainly die at least once as you orientate yourself in the darkness, and then possibly multiple more times as you try to navigate the maze-like corridors without getting stuck on the walls. Another section requires you to jump over small rocks and barriers but these look identical to other ones that have been area borders and impassable.

So, the game doesn’t carry through with what it succeeds at, and devolves into clunky action, but it does at least try to convey the scale and horror of the various Eldritch abominations within. The overall aesthetics of The Shore work well. The initial beachfront setting is barren, windswept, and atmospheric, the wreckage of ships are joined by the carcasses of dead sea creatures and everything is accompanied by the eerie cawing of seagulls. As you venture underground into the more surreal locations and come to confront the cosmic horror itself, the game does start to feel more generic and, as already noted, suffers from being so dark as to obscure much of the design work.

There is a very effective depiction of Dagon which successfully demonstrates how insignificant we are to such a creature and other tentacled leviathans can be seen in the far distance. It is when we ‘see’ Cthulhu that things fall apart though, as he stands like an angry rugby player and monologues about the fate of the world. This is so out of whack with everything that Cthulhu should be that I had to stop playing for a bit – particularly because the camera can be directed to linger on his shapely rear when he walks away.

I finished The Shore and unlocked the Platinum trophy in a little over two hours which is short even by the standards of walking simulators. When you consider that at least a quarter of that time consisted of repeating frustrating chase sequences and you have a game that manages to throw away all the good will it builds up with its atmospheric opening.

Summary

While there is some interest here for fans of cosmic horror and an atmospheric opening setting, The Shore is difficult to recommend for the frustrating second half.

Good

  • Moody and atmospheric first half
  • Some interesting (if unclear) puzzles
  • A novel take on some aspects of the Mythos

Bad

  • Second half of the game filled with frustrating chases
  • Laughable depiction of Cthulhu

Related stories from TSA



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/05/06/the-shore-enhanced-edition-review-cthulhu-goes-to-the-beach/

item.title: Directive 8020 Preload Date Guide & File Size, Explained
item.content:encoded:
It’s time for a fresh tale of terror, but this time in space! Supermassive Games is taking its auteur brand of interactive storytelling to the great unknown, and the Directive 8020 preload dates and the game’s file sizes have been revealed.

Telltale mastered the art of interactive video games, and Dispatch is a more recent example of the genre’s success. But when you think of masters of the craft, you can’t help but look at Supermassive Games.

Directive 8020 is the studio’s newest effort, so let’s learn more about it.

When Can You Preload Directive 8020?

You can start preloading Directive 8020 on PS5, Xbox Series X|S, and PC from May 10.

When it comes to preload information, one of the best sources around is PlayStation Game Size. They revealed the May 10 preload date on X, and as always, the process to preload is easy.

  • More Insider Gaming: Directive 8020 Release Date & Pre-Orders Now Live

Check out these steps:

  1. Choose if you want to play Directive 8020 on PlayStation, Xbox, or PC.
  2. Go to the platform’s storefront.
  3. Search for Directive 8020.
  4. Complete your purchase.
  5. Begin the preload on May 10.

If you do all of this correctly, you should have Directive 8020 ready to go on launch day!

All Directive 8020 File Sizes

Directive 8020 is 31.720 GB on the PS5, up to 40 GB on Steam, and approximately 60 GB on Xbox.

As seems to be the case, PlayStation 5 owners get the most optimized version, with PC not far behind, and Xbox owners just having to keep one eye on their storage.

  • More Insider Gaming: May 2026 Video Game Releases: Full Schedule and Biggest Games

I’ve provided a table featuring full details on all Directive 8020 platforms and the space needed to make it easier.

The Xbox details come from the official Directive 8020 store listing, the PC size is from the Steam page, and you can see PlayStation Game Size’s post below about PS5.

Have you ever played a Supermassive Games title before, such as Until Dawn or The Quarry? Could Directive 8020 be the best one yet? Let us know, and you can do so through the Insider Gaming Discord.

Check out 007 First Light’s Voice Actors, and the Forza Horizon 6 review embargo for more content.

Written by

Evergreen Editor

Andy is Insider Gaming's Senior Evergreen Editor and has been in the games journalism sphere in one way or another for over 7 years. His video game taste is as…

item.title: 007 First Light Sequel Depends On Public Reception
item.content:encoded:
The future of IO Interactive’s take on James Bond seems to be up in the air, as CEO Hakan Abrak revealed in an interview whether a 007 First Light sequel is on the cards.

Ultimately, it seems like it all boils down to how well received the first one is, with 007 First Light due to be released in just a few short weeks on May 27, 2026.

Hakan Abrak Seems Hopeful For A Sequel

In an interview with Christopher Dring of The Game Business, Hakan Abrak shared that his team are confident in the quality of 007 First Light, and so are Amazon, the current IP holder for the James Bond franchise.

“But from Amazon’s side, I’ll be so bold and say, they can see it’s a great game. And having invested and taken over that IP, they obviously want to make sure that everything that comes out is great. So, once they got into it and played, they became huge supporters. And we are very grateful for that.”

He goes on to mention how strong IO Interactive’s relationship is with Amazon MGM, and how they’re all looking towards the same future.

“There’s no doubt that we all want to see how this is doing. It’s not only Amazon MGM side, is also IO Interactive side. Is our character going to be well received? Is there going to be a community that likes him and wants to see more?”

Finally, he closes this segment of the interview off with a glimpse into the 007 future, and whether First Light could get a sequel, saying, “We are so blessed that we are in a place where we can pick and choose what we want to do. So, if this goes really well and it’s a beloved thing from the community, why not?”

Of course, what is defined as “really well” is up for interpretation, but it’s clear that IO Interactive has Amazon’s backing, so if 007 First Light sells well and receives a solid batch of reviews, we could very well see a sequel on the horizon.

Would you be open to a 007 First Light sequel? Share your thoughts in the official Insider Gaming Discord Server.

For more gaming news, check out the bizarre leaks for a new Worms game, as well as the new wave of layoffs at Build A Rocket Boy.

Charlie is one of Insider Gaming's Guides Writers who's been in the gaming industry for a little over a year. Unfortunately for him, he enjoys every video game genre out…

item.title: CWA Canada president says Saudi buyout of EA should trigger 'virtually every alarm bell'
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

May 6, 2026

4 Min Read

Labor union CWA Canada has called on the Canadian government to scrutinize the proposed $55 billion buyout of Electronic Arts over perceived national security risks. 

The Battlefield and EA Sports FC developer is being acquired by a consortium that includes Saudi Arabia's state-owned investment vehicle, Public Investment Fund (PIF), and private investment firms Silver Lake and Affinity Partners—the latter of which was established by Jared Kushner, the son-in-law of U.S. president Donald Trump. 

EA currently operates multiple studios across Canada in locations such as Quebec, Vancouver, Toronto, and Victoria.

In a letter sent to Canadian minister of industry Mélanie Joly on May 5, 2026, CWA Canada president Carmel Smyth said the union has "serious concerns" about the national security implications of the proposed takeover. 

"We are deeply concerned that a foreign sovereign wealth fund tasked with implementing the Saudi Arabian government's strategy to 'become the global hub for gaming and esports' plans to acquire one of the most successful North American video game publishers, with extensive operations in Canada, reaching hundreds of millions of players globally," reads the letter, shared with Game Developer via email. 

Related:EVE Online studio CCP Games turns independent and rebrands as Fenris Creations

"[...] In addition to the sheer size and strategy behind this deal, it is also highly relevant that, because the deal would put EA under the near-absolute control of PIF, not only would EA be closely economically integrated with the Saudi Arabian government, but control would effectively be solely in the hands of Crown Prince Mohammed Bin Salman, the unelected monarch of a country with, at best, a very different approach from Canada to personal autonomy and privacy and at worst a highly troubling record of human rights abuses including surveillance and misuse of information."

Advocacy groups such as Amnesty International and Human Rights Watch have strongly criticized Saudi Arabia's human rights record under the leadership of bin Salman, who they claim has suppressed freedom of speech by outlawing protests, criminalizing LGBTQ+ relationships and gender expression, and discriminating against women.

Bin Salman has also been linked to the 2018 murder of Washington Post journalist Jamal Khashoggi, and faced questions about his alleged involvement in the killing during a White House press conference in November 2025.

"The structure of the proposed acquisition puts the thousands of Canadian EA workers at increased risk"

In the letter, Smyth suggested the Saudi Arabian government "under the singular authority of Crown Prince Salman" is unlikely to operate EA in a way that is consistent with Canadian rights and values, and said the deal will allow the Saudi regime to collect and leverage the sensitive personal data of millions of Canadians.

Related:Xbox appoints new internal leaders and ditches Copilot AI

According to Smyth, that alone should be ringing "virtually every alarm bell regarding potential national security risks."

"Saudi Arabia could utilize its access to Canadians' personal information for purposes of mass surveillance, censorship, or propaganda, and we are highly skeptical Saudi Arabia would engage in these activities in a manner that is consistent with Canadian values," she continued. 

"Finally, because EA is heavily invested in developing artificial intelligence (AI), the transaction could also result in the transfer of sensitive technology to a foreign state, also an acute national security concern under the ICA." 

The 'ICA' refers to the Investment Canada Act, a Canadian federal law Smyth explained is designed to balance a positive investment climate while safeguarding Canada from "foreign actors that attempt to gain ownership or control of sensitive Canadian goods, technology, infrastructure, or personal data" for nefarious reasons.

Smyth claimed the EA buyout fits that profile—but she has other concerns over how the deal might impact the livelihoods of workers at the global publisher. 

Related:Valve to restock Steam Controller after it sells out in a day

"Workers in the video game industry have experienced significant layoffs in recent years, and the structure of the proposed acquisition puts the thousands of Canadian EA workers at increased risk," she continued. "The deal is being financed with US$20 billion in debt; as a result, some analysts suggest layoffs are one of the key ways the company will seek to cut costs in order to service its massive debt."

EA previously suggested the buyout won't result in "immediate" job cuts. It has since cut a number of roles across Battlefield Studios. The company also pledged to foster a culture of 'creativity, pioneering, passion, determination, learning, and teamwork' under its new owners. 

Even so, Smyth fears the Saudi Arabian state—which will own 93.4 percent of EA if the deal is approved—might begin using EA titles as vehicles for "mass surveillance and state-sponsored propaganda." As a result, she has urged the Canadian government to "blow the whistle" on the deal. 

This isn't the first time the buyout has been called into question. Last year, a group of unionized EA employees argued the proposed acquisition will likely harm workers and called on regulators in the United States to place it under the microscope. 

U.S. senators Richard Blumenthal and Elizabeth Warren also expressed concerns about the national security risks posed by the take-private. In January, another group of U.S. lawmakers said the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) must review the acquisition over fears it could incentive layoffs, offshoring, studio closures, and other cost-cutting measures.

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/union-president-says-saudi-buyout-of-ea-should-trigger-virtually-every-alarm-bell-

item.title: Thief, Dishonored and Philip Marlowe combine in Noirmancer, a stealth sim that lets you wipe minds with a snap of your fingers
item.content:encoded:
Oh heck, colleagues, we got one: a promising videogame. Noirmancer is a first-person single-player stealth sim, heavily inspired by the original Thief, in which you are a hard-boiled sleuth with supernatural abilities. Think Humphrey Bogart, if Humphrey Bogart were a Dishonored character. Here are some things you can do: swim in midair, transform into vapour, stun alerted guards with pew-pew gestures, and chuck down telekinetic mats to serve as (usefully sound-absorbing) platforms.

It might sound like a jumble, like one of those Marvel comicbook movesets they have to prune severely for the sake of a coherent film adaptation, but the whimsical stage magician hand animations and midnight jazz SFX absolutely sell the package. Instead of swinging a blackjack (noirjack?), you'll put mooks to sleep with a snap of your gloved fingers and a rattle of cymbals. Forget Bogart - this is Penn or Teller, whichever of them has springier knees.

That said, you'll often fall back on more elementary, non-magical stealth gambits, like opening windows slowly to minimise noise, eavesdropping for hints, and identifying shadowy paths through well-lit areas using your visibility indicator in bottom right. All this takes place in a city after sundown, a mildly synesthetic world of white outlines and rippling footsteps. The missions offer a variety of safes to crack and filing cabinets to rifle through; if you're extra nosy, you might find ways to exploit "the back-door dealings of important people".

There's a 2-4 hour alpha demo for Noirmancer on Itch.io, with a full Steam release to follow (no date yet). One goal of the alpha is to test out what developer DistractedMOSFET considers to be Noirmancer's boldest idea: "a stealth game where you MUST erase memories in order to de-alert the situation."

I've had a quick play of the demo, and I'm still making my mind up about the memory wipe stuff. I'm currently preoccupied with using telekinetic mats for parkouring purposes, which reminds me of wielding the foam gun in Arkane's Prey. Come to think of it, this game could use a Mimic power, if it doesn't already have one. I would like to be a fedora in this game. Perhaps one of those roaming guards could absent-mindedly put me on.

Prefer to infiltrate in twos or threes? Please rub this piece of innocent blank paper with your cursor. It harbours the release date for Thick as Thieves, the new stealth sim from Warren Spector and Otherside.



https://www.rockpapershotgun.com/thief-dishonored-and-philip-marlowe-combine-in-noirmancer-a-stealth-sim-that-lets-you-wipe-minds-with-a-snap-of-your-fingers
